one_line_takeaway: Perfect-retraining-style machine-unlearning definitions let an attacker who controls as few as omega(1) data points reconstruct almost an entire otherwise-DP-protected dataset purely through the sequence of deletion requests, motivating a new simulation-based 'undeleted-safe' definition that instead protects the privacy of everyone who did NOT ask to be deleted.
key_finding: Any unlearning definition satisfied by perfect retraining (which includes essentially all mainstream unlearning definitions surveyed, Cao & Yang 2015 through Gupta et al. 2021, plus Cohen et al. 2023a and Godin & Lamontagne 2021) permits an attacker controlling merely omega(1) points to reconstruct almost the entire dataset (error o(n)) via a task that is otherwise solvable with (o(1), o(1))-differential privacy in the single-shot (no-deletion) setting -- i.e., deletions themselves are the leakage channel, not the underlying computation. Conversely, the privacy-oriented definitions that resist this attack (Garg et al. 2020, Gao et al. 2022, Chourasia & Shah 2023) are shown to disallow simple, practically important functionalities like exact sums or bulletin boards; Chourasia & Shah's per-timestep adaptive definition is additionally shown vulnerable to a newly constructed 'generalized differencing attack' (Section 2.2) even though it individually randomizes each release. The authors' new 'undeleted-safe' definition resolves this tension: it is satisfied by any stateless algorithm (enabling exact sum and bulletin board, Example 4.1) and, via a differentially-private range-query data structure, by an approximate-median mechanism (Example 4.3) whose error does not grow with the number of deletions k -- in contrast to the classical continual-observation 'tree algorithm' of Dwork et al. (2010), which the authors note provably requires error scaling as Omega(log k) (citing Cohen et al. 2024).
core_contribution: The paper shows that machine-unlearning security definitions built around 'perfect retraining' (mimicking a model retrained from scratch without the deleted point) are fundamentally unsafe for the REMAINING (undeleted) data: for a specific family of tasks that can be solved with strong differential privacy in a single-shot release, an attacker who controls only omega(1) data points can reconstruct almost the entire dataset merely by issuing a carefully chosen sequence of deletion requests and observing the resulting outputs (Theorem 1.1, formalized as Theorem B.2/B.3 and Corollary B.4 via a 'batch queries' (BQ) problem built on Dwork & Yekhanin's 2008 statistical-disclosure reconstruction attack). The authors then survey existing unlearning definitions, classifying them into (a) those satisfied by perfect retraining and hence vulnerable to this attack (Cao & Yang 2015, Ginart et al. 2019, Guo et al. 2019, Sekhari et al. 2021, Gupta et al. 2021, and the 'deletion-as-control' definition of Cohen et al. 2023a, and Godin & Lamontagne 2021), and (b) privacy-oriented definitions that avoid the attack but are too restrictive to support basic functionalities such as exact summation or a bulletin board (Garg et al. 2020's 'deletion-as-compliance', its relaxation by Gao et al. 2022, and the adaptive-adversary definition of Chourasia & Shah 2023 -- the last of which the authors additionally show is vulnerable to a new 'generalized differencing attack' they construct in Section 2.2). As the core positive contribution, the paper proposes a new family of security definitions ('k-undeleted-safe', non-adaptive/static-adaptive/dynamic-adaptive variants, Definitions 3.1/3.3/3.4, plus a bounded-leakage relaxation Definition 4.2) that specifically protects undeleted points' privacy via a simulation-based game, and demonstrates the definition is compatible with useful functionalities: stateless algorithms trivially satisfy it (exact sum, bulletin board via Example 4.1), and a differentially-private range-query data structure can support an approximate median that also satisfies the definition (Example 4.3), leading to a general recipe (Section 4.2) applicable to any function expressible in Cao & Yang's non-adaptive statistical-query (SQ) framework.
threat_model: A curator holds a dataset D and answers a sequence of data-deletion requests, releasing an updated output after each deletion (this models an unlearning API/pipeline, not raw model weights). The attacker controls a subset of the data points (statically fixed in advance, or chosen adaptively/dynamically during the interaction, per the three definition variants) and can request their own controlled points be deleted; it observes the full sequence of outputs before and after each deletion (plus optional side information about non-controlled points). The attacker's goal is to reconstruct or infer values of points it does NOT control, i.e., points belonging to other users who never requested deletion. This is a purely information-theoretic/black-box query threat model (no gradient/weight access, no assumptions about model architecture) -- it applies to any unlearning mechanism, ML-based or otherwise, that satisfies a 'perfect retraining' style definition.
limitations: The work is purely theoretical/cryptographic: no evaluation on real ML unlearning systems, no deep-learning benchmarks, and only one small illustrative empirical demonstration (synthetic 1-D k-means with two clusters). The bounded-leakage relaxation (Definition 4.2) requires a designer-chosen leakage function g with no general method for selecting or minimizing it -- the paper only states that the trivial choice g=D makes the definition vacuous and g=empty recovers the strict definition. The positive 'recipe' for constructing undeleted-safe algorithms (Section 4.2) is explicitly restricted to functions expressible in Cao & Yang's non-adaptive statistical-query (SQ) framework with a small predicate collection C; it is not claimed to extend to general (e.g., non-convex, iterative, or non-SQ) learning algorithms such as deep neural network unlearning. The paper explicitly declines to address two other well-known unlearning privacy risks: leakage inherent to the functionality f itself (analogous to MPC's 'nothing beyond f' guarantee) and leakage about the deleted point itself via differencing attacks, treating both as out of scope / accepted risk that a user consents to when requesting deletion.

datasets_used: None (synthetic constructions only): multiset T = D ∪ S with D a random n/2-size subset of [n] and S consisting of copies of a special symbol/element (CountMod and batch-queries constructions); synthetic 1-D points drawn from a uniform distribution over an interval and from a mixture of two normal distributions for the k-means empirical demonstration in Appendix A.
quantitative_results: Theorem 1.1 / Corollary B.4: there is a task solvable with (o(1), o(1))-differential privacy in the single-shot setting, but for which any perfect-retraining unlearning mechanism lets an attacker controlling |B| = omega(1) points reconstruct a set D-hat with symmetric-difference error |D △ D-hat| ≤ o(n) (i.e., almost all of the ~n-point dataset), with success probability at least 1 - beta.

Theorem B.2: an (epsilon, delta)-DP one-shot algorithm exists for the batch-queries (BQ) problem when k ≥ (2/epsilon) log(2/beta), t = o(epsilon^2 n / log(1/delta)), and n = omega(log(1/delta) log(1/beta) / epsilon^2), achieving average squared error alpha = O(log(1/delta) · (t + sqrt(t log(1/beta)) + log(1/beta)) / epsilon^2) = o(n).

Example 4.1 / 4.3 comparison (qualitative, no numeric table): a stateless single-DP-noise-addition summation/approximate-median mechanism achieves error independent of the number of deletions k, versus the classical continual-observation 'tree algorithm' (Dwork et al. 2010) which the authors state provably requires error scaling as Omega(log k) (per Cohen et al. 2024).
baselines_compared: Prior unlearning definitions surveyed and shown vulnerable to the reconstruction/differencing attacks (not empirical baselines, but the definitions the paper's attack theorem applies to): Cao & Yang (2015), Ginart et al. (2019), Guo et al. (2019), Sekhari et al. (2021), Gupta et al. (2021), Cohen et al. (2023a) 'deletion-as-control', Godin & Lamontagne (2021).

Prior privacy-oriented definitions shown to be too restrictive (disallow exact sum / bulletin board): Garg et al. (2020) 'deletion-as-compliance', Gao et al. (2022) relaxation of Garg et al., Chourasia & Shah (2023) adaptive-adversary definition (further shown vulnerable to the paper's new generalized differencing attack).
